topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled manuscript lay on the coffee table, a thick stack of paper adorned with my own face, my quirks, my most mundane moments, all meticulously chronicled in prose. I froze mid-sip of my lukewarm tea, the ceramic mug suddenly feeling hot against my palms. Across the room, Liam was humming, oblivious, his brow furrowed in concentration as he highlighted a passage describing my disastrous attempt at baking sourdough. My cheeks flushed a deep, alarming crimson, and I quickly tried to shove the papers under a throw blanket, my movements jerky and clumsy. "What are you doing?" Liam looked up, a bright, easy smile on his face, completely missing the seismic shift in the atmosphere. "Oh, nothing," I managed, my voice coming out several octaves higher than intended. "Just... organizing."

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked inside a seldom-used drawer—a bound notebook filled with elaborate descriptions of my moods and habits, woven into a narrative structure. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I snatched the book, my fingers trembling slightly as I flipped through pages detailing my awkward attempts at public speaking and my inexplicable fondness for rainy afternoons. When I presented it to Sarah, my best friend, my hands shook so badly I had to steady myself on the bookshelf. "He's... he's writing a book about me," I whispered, the words tasting metallic on my tongue. Sarah blinked slowly, her expression shifting from curiosity to utter bewilderment. "About you? What on earth are you talking about?" I immediately looked down at my shoes, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

It was the late afternoon quiet that brought the discovery. I walked into the study, intending to find a book, and stopped dead when I saw the open laptop displaying a meticulously formatted chapter titled, "The Unintentional Comedy of Clara." My breath hitched. The words detailed my morning routine, the way I chewed my pen when I was thinking deeply, even the specific shade of anxiety I exhibited before a big meeting. A wave of intense self-consciousness washed over me, making my skin prickle. I backed away slowly, my eyes darting around the room as if expecting the words to jump off the screen and critique me. When Mark finally called out, asking if I was alright, I forced a casual shrug, trying to smooth down my shirt, acutely aware of the weight of the story unfolding on that glowing rectangle.
